title: Building plan needed for solar installation
description: GEORGE NEWS - A new requirement for solar...
bodyText:
GEORGE NEWS - A new requirement for solar system installations which came into effect on 1 July is the submission of a building plan to the George Municipality's Building Control section.


> This new rule has some homeowners who are considering going solar hot under the collar because of resultant added bureaucratic red tape and higher costs.


However, local specialists urge that the new rule is being introduced for safety's sake. A structural engineer registered with the Engineering Council of South Africa (Ecsa) has to sign off on all installations. According to local structural engineer Marius van Coller, there is a reason for an engineer's inspection. He says there is a risk of structural collapse if the roof truss design is not adequate for the number of solar panels being installed.

"Having professionals sign off on the installation gives the homeowner peace of mind and it also ensures that you are covered with your insurer."

Certificate of complianceAn electrical certificate of compliance (CoC) must also be issued by an accredited installer. If you use an accredited installer, chances are that they will take care of the solar application process on your behalf.

According to Gerrie Rossouw of Specialized Solar Systems (SSS), they use the services of two Ecsa-registered engineers to sign off on all their projects and the engineer's fee is included in SSS' fee to the customer.


> "We are registered as an electrical contractor so we are able to supply a valid CoC upon completion. After permission to install is received from the municipality, we proceed with the installation.


"Once completed, we conduct an inspection with the Ecsa engineer and when he approves, we upload the required final single-line diagram and CoC on the municipality's online portal, as required. Once approved, we receive the permission to generate and we then switch on the system," says Rossouw.

It's law, but there are added costsAccording to George Municipality, the National Building Regulations and Building Standards Act requires submission of a building plan. The municipality wants the application to be submitted within three months of the time permission is granted for the solar installation.


> This is where the homeowner coughs up additional rands. The building application tariff should be below R1 000, but hiring an architectural technologist or architect to submit the plan could cost a further few thousand rand.


Marius Smith, owner of M Smith Architectural Group, says in cases where no strengthening of the roof is needed, the architectural fee would be a minimum of R5 000, but it could increase above R10 000 if the roof truss design has to be amended.


> The architectural fees include a mandatory survey of the sewage layout on the property that must be incorporated onto the building plan. The latter is an additional requirement of the Building Control office, according to Smith.


The municipality pointed out that the Act states that "any facilities or system, or part or portion thereof, within or outside but incidental to a building, for the provision of a water supply, drainage, sewerage, stormwater disposal, electricity supply or other similar service in respect of the building" need an approved building plan. This can be temporary or permanent and irrespective of the materials used. If it is in connection with the "accommodation or convenience of human beings or animals", it needs a plan.

Click below for a related article:

What must a homeowner do before and after installing solar panels?

‘We bring you the latest Garden Route, Hessequa, Karoo news’
